words.usage: Fame on social media is often ephemeral — viral today, forgotten by Friday.
words.note: Greek hēmera = day. Literally 'lasting a day'.

words.usage: His laconic reply — 'Fine.' — ended the negotiation in one word.
words.note: From Laconia (Sparta). Spartans were famously brief.

words.usage: Despite the mock-test dip, she remained sanguine about her percentile.
words.note: Latin sanguis = blood. Old medicine linked blood to cheerfulness.

words.usage: The fine print seemed designed to obfuscate the actual interest rate.
words.note: Latin fuscus = dark. To darken the meaning.

words.usage: A gregarious student, he knew every batch from A2701 to Zebar.
words.note: Latin grex = flock. One who stays with the herd.

idioms.caption: Take it literally and you'd need a very strong umbrella.
idioms.origin: Origin is murky — one theory points to 17th-century storms washing stray animals through London streets.
idioms.usage: The match was abandoned because it was raining cats and dogs.

idioms.caption: One clumsy jar, and every bean is public knowledge.
idioms.origin: Possibly from ancient Greek voting with beans — a tipped jar revealed the result early.
idioms.usage: Under pressure from the class, he finally spilled the beans about the surprise test.

words.usage: His quixotic plan to finish all 31 tests in one weekend collapsed by Sunday noon.
words.note: From Don Quixote — the knight who fought windmills.

words.usage: The taciturn topper let her scorecard do the talking.
words.note: Latin tacere = to be silent. Cousin of 'tacit'.

words.usage: Smartphones are so ubiquitous that a no-phone classroom feels historic.
words.note: Latin ubique = everywhere.

words.usage: Magnanimous in victory, she coached the very students who had beaten her last year.
words.note: Latin magnus (great) + animus (spirit) = great-souled.

options: A sign of things to come
words.usage: Falling accuracy in RC is often a harbinger of burnout, not weakness.
words.note: Originally a scout sent ahead to book lodgings for an army.

idioms.caption: A hammer, a block of ice, and suddenly everyone can swim.
idioms.origin: From ships that broke frozen rivers so trade could begin — clearing the way for others.
idioms.usage: A quick round of introductions broke the ice on the first day of the IPMAT batch.

idioms.caption: Feet so cold they refuse to walk you into the exam hall.
options: Sudden loss of nerve before something
idioms.origin: Likely from 19th-century gamblers who claimed frozen feet to escape a losing table.
idioms.usage: He had the application ready but got cold feet just before hitting submit.

words.usage: The essay was grammatically perfect but insipid — not one memorable line.
words.note: Latin in (not) + sapidus (tasty). Literally flavourless.

words.usage: A garrulous seatmate can cost you two RC passages' worth of focus.
words.note: Latin garrire = to chatter. Opposite of Day 2's taciturn.

options: Given to sudden changes of mood
words.usage: The capricious paper-setters swung from easy vocab to brutal RC in one year.
words.note: From Italian capriccio — a sudden start, like a goat's leap.

options: Understood by only a select few
words.usage: Weinstein Stage Analysis sounds esoteric until someone draws you the four stages.
words.note: Greek esōterikos = inner circle. Knowledge for insiders.

words.usage: More mock tests are useful, but they're no panacea for weak fundamentals.
words.note: Greek goddess Panakeia — 'all-healing'.

idioms.idiom: Let the cat out of the bag
idioms.caption: The bag had one job.
options: To reveal a secret by mistake
idioms.origin: One theory: market cheats sold 'pigs' in sacks that turned out to be cats — until someone opened the bag.
idioms.usage: The surprise farewell was ruined when Rohan let the cat out of the bag in the group chat.

idioms.caption: Before electricity, late-night study literally burned oil.
options: To work or study late into the night
idioms.origin: From the era of oil lamps — staying up late meant literally spending oil.
idioms.usage: The week before IPMAT, half the batch was burning the midnight oil.

words.usage: A perfunctory revision — flipping pages without testing yourself — fools no exam.
words.note: Latin perfungi = to get through. Just getting it done.

words.usage: His obsequious praise of the interviewer earned a raised eyebrow, not a seat.
words.note: Latin obsequi = to comply. Fawning like a courtier.

words.usage: The exam hall's cacophony of coughs and clicking pens tested everyone's focus.
words.note: Greek kakos (bad) + phōnē (sound). Opposite: euphony.

words.usage: An iconoclast in the coaching world, she banned rote word-lists entirely.
words.note: Greek 'image-breaker' — literally destroyers of religious icons.

words.usage: Her penchant for etymology turned every new word into a detective case.
words.note: French pencher = to lean. What you lean towards.

idioms.caption: The moon, feeling experimental.
idioms.origin: A 'blue moon' is the rare second full moon in one calendar month.
idioms.usage: He checks the leaderboard once in a blue moon — the toppers check it hourly.

idioms.idiom: Cost an arm and a leg
idioms.caption: The price tag nobody wants to pay in body parts.
idioms.origin: Popularised after WWII, when losing limbs was the ultimate price a soldier could pay.
idioms.usage: Good coaching shouldn't cost an arm and a leg — that's half the point of this portal.

words.usage: Both sides stayed intransigent, and the group project quietly died.
words.note: Latin in + transigere (to settle) — one who won't settle.

words.usage: The RJ's mellifluous voice made even traffic updates sound like poetry.
words.note: Latin mel (honey) + fluere (to flow). Honey-flowing.

words.usage: The 3 p.m. lecture on clauses proved dangerously soporific.
words.note: Latin sopor = deep sleep. A lullaby in adjective form.

words.usage: Guesswork without elimination is anathema to a serious test-taker.
words.note: Greek — originally a formal curse of the church.

words.usage: In investing, the dilettante buys tips; the student of charts buys stages.
words.note: Italian dilettare = to delight. One who dabbles for pleasure.

idioms.idiom: Hit the nail on the head
idioms.caption: One clean strike. No bent nails, no bruised thumbs.
idioms.origin: From carpentry — the perfect strike drives the nail straight home.
idioms.usage: When she said the para's tone was 'wry, not bitter', she hit the nail on the head.

idioms.caption: The squirrel left this tree twenty minutes ago.
options: Pursuing a mistaken line of thought
idioms.origin: From hunting dogs that cornered prey — and kept barking at trees the animal had already fled.
idioms.usage: If you're blaming vocabulary for your RC score, you may be barking up the wrong tree.

words.usage: The truculent debater attacked every question instead of answering any.
words.note: Latin trux = fierce, savage.

words.usage: A sycophant agrees with the boss's typo; a colleague quietly fixes it.
words.note: Greek sykophantēs — originally an informer in Athens.

words.usage: The recalcitrant printer chose exam morning to reject every command.
words.note: Latin recalcitrare = to kick back, like a mule.

words.usage: A pellucid argument needs no second reading — the logic shows straight through.
words.note: Latin per + lucere (to shine). Light passes through.

options: A lover of solitude by faith
words.usage: He plays the misanthrope online but remembers every student's birthday.
words.note: Greek misein (hate) + anthrōpos (human).

idioms.caption: Hitting everything except the point.
idioms.origin: From bird hunting — beaters struck around bushes to flush birds out, while others made the actual catch.
idioms.usage: Stop beating around the bush and tell me: did the mock go badly?

idioms.caption: A personal rain cloud with excellent aim.
idioms.origin: Nautical origin — sick sailors were sent below deck, literally under the weather.
idioms.usage: She skipped the speed test because she was feeling under the weather.

words.usage: The vociferous protests over the answer key forced a re-evaluation.
words.note: Latin vox (voice) + ferre (to carry). Voice-carrying.

words.usage: She felt ambivalent about the drop year — freedom and fear in equal parts.
words.note: Latin ambi (both) + valere (to be strong). Pulled both ways.

words.usage: Fear of being ostracized keeps many students from asking 'basic' doubts.
words.note: From Greek ostrakon — pottery shards used to vote citizens into exile.

words.usage: A ten-minute error-log review does more to ameliorate scores than three fresh mocks.
words.note: Latin melior = better. Same root as 'meliorism'.

words.usage: The pragmatic move isn't the perfect plan — it's the one you'll actually follow.
words.note: Greek pragma = deed, action.

idioms.caption: Good news wearing a very unconvincing moustache.
options: A misfortune that turns out beneficial
idioms.origin: The phrase appears in an 18th-century hymn — hardship as heaven's hidden favour.
idioms.usage: Missing that college was a blessing in disguise; the drop year rebuilt her fundamentals.

idioms.idiom: The ball is in your court
idioms.caption: The ball has crossed the net. Everyone is watching you.
options: It's now your turn to act or decide
idioms.origin: Straight from tennis — once the ball lands on your side, only you can play it.
idioms.usage: I've shared the study plan and the portal login. The ball is in your court now.

words.usage: She accepted the extra mock test with surprising alacrity.
words.note: Latin alacer = lively, eager.

words.usage: The topper walked out of the exam hall ebullient, high-fiving strangers.
words.note: Latin bullire = to boil. Enthusiasm bubbling over.

options: Very attentive to detail; hard to please
words.usage: A fastidious editor, she caught the single misplaced comma on page 40.
words.note: Latin fastidium = loathing. Originally 'easily disgusted'.

options: Looking or sounding sad and gloomy
words.usage: His lugubrious face after the mock made everyone assume the worst.
words.note: Latin lugere = to mourn.

words.usage: The nefarious scheme involved leaking fake answer keys to rivals.
words.note: Latin nefas = a wrong, a crime against divine law.

idioms.caption: No anaesthesia in old field hospitals — just something to clench.
options: Face a painful situation with courage
idioms.origin: Before anaesthesia, soldiers reportedly bit on a bullet during surgery to endure the pain.
idioms.usage: I hate grammar drills, but I'll bite the bullet and finish the modifier deck tonight.

idioms.caption: The towel flies, the fight ends.
idioms.origin: From boxing — a trainer throws a towel into the ring to stop the fight and concede.
idioms.usage: Half the batch wanted to throw in the towel after Test 18; nobody actually did.

words.usage: Reticent in class, he wrote the sharpest essays in the batch.
words.note: Latin tacere = to be silent (same root as taciturn).

words.usage: The topic sounded prosaic — 'My Daily Routine' — until she made it a thriller.
words.note: Latin prosa = straightforward speech. Prose, not poetry.

words.usage: Students venerate toppers a little too much; toppers are just students with routines.
words.note: Latin venerari = to worship.

words.usage: He became a vocabulary zealot — correcting even the teacher's synonyms.
words.note: Greek zelotes = emulous follower. Originally a Jewish resistance sect.

words.usage: The debate ended in acrimony, with both sides refusing to shake hands.
words.note: Latin acer = sharp. Sharpness of temper.

idioms.caption: No legs are harmed in the making of this joke.
options: To tease or joke with someone
idioms.origin: Possibly from Victorian street thieves tripping victims — the meaning softened into harmless teasing.
idioms.usage: Relax, there's no surprise test tomorrow — I was just pulling your leg.

idioms.caption: Nine clouds up, gravity optional.
idioms.origin: One theory credits the US Weather Bureau's cloud classification, where 'nine' was the towering cumulonimbus — the highest of all.
idioms.usage: She's been on cloud nine since the IPMAT call letter arrived.

options: Hostile and aggressive; ready to fight
words.usage: One belligerent comment in the group chat derailed the whole study plan.
words.note: Latin bellum = war. One waging war.

words.usage: Her candid feedback stung, but it fixed my RC approach in a week.
words.note: Latin candidus = white, pure. Honest = unstained.

words.usage: Out of deference to the seniors, he let them pick the mock schedule.
words.note: Latin deferre = to yield, carry down.

words.usage: Careful — this word is a classic trap. Three back-to-back mocks will enervate anyone.
words.note: Latin nervus = sinew. To cut the sinews — the opposite of energise.

words.usage: A fortuitous seat swap put her next to the batch's best note-maker.
words.note: Latin fors = chance, luck.

idioms.caption: So easy you could eat it.
idioms.origin: Popularised by 1930s American slang; possibly from cakewalk contests where the prize for an easy stroll was a cake.
idioms.usage: After the RC drills, the actual exam passage felt like a piece of cake.

idioms.caption: Exactly what the fire wanted.
idioms.origin: An image as old as fire itself — Roman writers used the phrase two thousand years ago.
idioms.usage: Bringing up last year's result only added fuel to the fire during the argument.

words.usage: 'Hard work is the key to success' — a hackneyed line that kills essay scores.
words.note: From Hackney horses — hired out so often they wore out.

words.usage: An impetuous guess on a negative-marking question costs more than a skip.
words.note: Latin impetus = attack, rush.

options: To place side by side for contrast
words.usage: The passage juxtaposes a monk's silence with a trader's chaos — that contrast IS the answer.
words.note: Latin juxta = next to + French poser = to place.

words.usage: Post-lunch lectures make everyone lethargic; that's when attendance tricks fail.
words.note: Greek lethargos = forgetful, from Lethe, the river of forgetfulness.

options: Prone to sudden, unpredictable changes of mood
words.usage: His mercurial moods meant one mock decided whether the week was good or ruined.
words.note: From Mercury — swift messenger god, and the liquid metal that never sits still.

idioms.caption: The band plays whether you like the tune or not.
options: Accept the consequences of your actions
idioms.origin: One theory: disgraced soldiers were drummed out of the regiment, literally facing the band.
idioms.usage: He skipped three tests; now he has to face the music in the mentoring session.

idioms.caption: The milk is on the floor. The floor is not giving it back.
options: Waste time regretting what cannot be undone
idioms.origin: A proverb since the 1600s — spilt milk cannot be gathered up again.
idioms.usage: The paper's done. Don't cry over spilt milk; analyse it and move to the next mock.

words.usage: He gave a nonchalant shrug at the result, then quietly topped the next test.
words.note: French non + chaloir = to not be heated about it.

words.usage: An ostentatious vocabulary impresses no examiner; precision does.
words.note: Latin ostentare = to display.

words.usage: A quick apology placated the invigilator after the phone buzzed.
words.note: Latin placare = to soothe.

words.usage: A querulous tone in the passage signals the author disapproves — mark it.
words.note: Latin queri = to complain. Not related to 'query'!

words.usage: 'Return back' and 'repeat again' are redundant — the verb already says it.
words.note: Latin redundare = to overflow.

idioms.caption: Everyone sees it. Nobody mentions it.
options: An obvious problem no one wants to discuss
idioms.origin: Traced to a 19th-century Russian fable about a man who tours a museum and notices everything except the elephant.
idioms.usage: Attendance is the elephant in the room — everyone blames the syllabus instead.

idioms.caption: Borrowed weather, unreturned.
options: Take credit or attention meant for another
idioms.origin: Playwright John Dennis invented a thunder machine for his play; rivals used it in Macbeth, and he raged that they'd stolen his thunder.
idioms.usage: Announcing your score during her farewell speech? That's stealing her thunder.

options: A happy accident; luck in finding good things
words.usage: Pure serendipity: the essay topic was the exact article she'd read that morning.
words.note: Coined by Horace Walpole from the Persian tale 'The Three Princes of Serendip'.

words.usage: Tenacious students treat a 40th percentile as data, not destiny.
words.note: Latin tenere = to hold.

words.usage: Don't vacillate between two options for ninety seconds — mark, move, return.
words.note: Latin vacillare = to sway.

options: Full of gentle longing or yearning
words.usage: The passage's wistful tone about 'lost letter-writing' hints at the author's view.
words.note: From obsolete 'wistly' = intently; shaded toward longing.

words.usage: The hostel room was austere — a bed, a desk, and a poster of the IIM gate.
words.note: Greek austeros = making the tongue dry, harsh.

idioms.caption: Someone has to argue for the horns.
options: Argue the opposite side to test an idea
idioms.origin: From the Vatican's advocatus diaboli — an official appointed to argue against a candidate's sainthood.
idioms.usage: I agree with your answer, but let me play devil's advocate: what if the tone is ironic?

idioms.caption: The balloon has opinions now.
idioms.origin: From horse-driving — losing grip of the reins meant losing control of the horses.
idioms.usage: The group-study meme thread got out of hand; nobody studied after 9 pm.

words.usage: Brevity wins in para-summary: the shortest option that keeps the core often survives.
words.note: Latin brevis = short. 'Brevity is the soul of wit' — Hamlet.

words.usage: The distractor options use chicanery — half-true words arranged to mislead.
words.note: French chicaner = to quibble in legal disputes.

options: A sudden, complete failure; a fiasco
words.usage: Attempting all 100 questions with negative marking was a predictable debacle.
words.note: French débâcler = to unbar; originally the violent breaking of river ice.

words.usage: He had the effrontery to ask for grace marks after skipping every class.
words.note: Latin effrons = barefaced, without shame on the forehead.

words.usage: A frugal test-taker spends time only where marks grow.
words.note: Latin frux = fruit, profit. Spending only what bears fruit.

idioms.idiom: Kill two birds with one stone
idioms.caption: Efficient. The birds disagree.
options: Achieve two goals with one action
idioms.origin: An old proverb of efficiency, in English print since the 1600s.
idioms.usage: Reading editorials kills two birds with one stone — vocabulary and current affairs.

idioms.caption: Comfortable? No. Committed? Also no.
idioms.origin: The image of someone perched between two fields, refusing to drop into either, dates to 19th-century American politics.
idioms.usage: On this debate topic, sitting on the fence scores zero — pick a side and argue.

words.usage: Gullible readers accept the first plausible option; trained ones test it against the passage.
words.note: From gull (to dupe) — the bird was thought foolishly easy to catch.

words.usage: Her histrionic reaction to a one-mark loss entertained the whole batch.
words.note: Latin histrio = actor.

words.usage: An indolent revision plan — 'I'll do it all in May' — is how percentiles die.
words.note: Latin in + dolere = feeling no pain; drifted to 'taking no trouble'.

words.usage: The jubilant Zebar corridor after results needed its own noise policy.
words.note: Latin jubilare = to shout for joy.

options: To express deep grief or sorrow
words.usage: The author laments the decline of deep reading — that single verb sets the tone question.
words.note: Latin lamentum = a wailing.

idioms.caption: Bulk discount on ordinary.
options: Very common and of little value
idioms.origin: American phrase from the 1800s, when a dime genuinely bought a dozen eggs.
idioms.usage: Generic essay openings are a dime a dozen; a sharp first line is not.

idioms.caption: The road ends. You don't.
idioms.origin: From the Sermon on the Mount — 'whosoever shall compel thee to go a mile, go with him twain'.
idioms.usage: She goes the extra mile: every wrong answer gets a written post-mortem.

words.usage: A meticulous error log beats ten extra mocks.
words.note: Latin metus = fear — originally 'fearful', then 'carefully timid about mistakes'.

words.usage: The essay drips with nostalgia for handwritten letters — that's your tone answer.
words.note: Greek nostos = homecoming + algos = pain. Once diagnosed as a disease in soldiers.

words.usage: The opulent farmhouse in the RC contrasts with the narrator's one-room flat — contrast = question.
words.note: Latin ops = wealth, resources.

options: Excessively concerned with minor rules and details
words.usage: Correcting 'less marks' to 'fewer marks' mid-condolence is peak pedantic.
words.note: From pedant — originally just a schoolmaster.

options: A state of uncertainty; a dilemma
words.usage: Classic quandary: attempt the long RC for 20 marks or bank the short one for 12.
words.note: Origin uncertain — possibly from Latin quando = when, as in 'what now, when?'

idioms.caption: The wind now owns your caution.
idioms.origin: The image of tossing something away on the breeze — recorded in English since the 1600s.
idioms.usage: In the last five minutes he threw caution to the wind and guessed all pending questions.

idioms.caption: No way back. That was the point.
options: Destroy relationships or options for return
idioms.origin: From military strategy — armies burned bridges behind them so retreat was impossible.
idioms.usage: Leave the coaching politely; never burn bridges with mentors you may need again.

words.usage: Years later, the rancor over that group-project betrayal still surfaces at reunions.
words.note: Latin rancere = to stink, to be rotten.

words.usage: She stayed stoic through three server crashes during the online mock.
words.note: From the Stoa (porch) in Athens where Zeno taught calm endurance.

options: Fear or anxiety about what may happen
words.usage: He opened the scorecard with trepidation and closed it with relief.
words.note: Latin trepidare = to tremble.

options: Offence or annoyance taken at something
words.usage: She took umbrage at being called 'lucky' — it dismissed two years of 6 am revisions.
words.note: Latin umbra = shadow. To feel overshadowed, slighted.

options: Having a huge appetite; devouring greedily
words.usage: A voracious reader, she finished the entire RC compendium before Week 3.
words.note: Latin vorare = to devour.

idioms.caption: The camel had limits. Who knew.
options: The final problem that makes a situation unbearable
idioms.origin: From the proverb 'the straw that broke the camel's back' — one tiny addition to an already crushing load.
idioms.usage: The fourth 'urgent' schedule change was the last straw; the batch revolted.

idioms.caption: The real message never got typed.
options: Understand the hidden or implied meaning
idioms.origin: From cryptography — secret messages were once written in invisible ink between the visible lines.
idioms.usage: Inference questions are exactly this: read between the lines, but never beyond them.

words.usage: Be wary of options with 'always' and 'never' — extremes rarely survive the passage.
words.note: Old English waer = aware, on guard.

words.usage: The topper found the adulation exhausting — forty juniors asking for 'the one trick'.
words.note: Latin adulari = to fawn like a dog.

words.usage: Bombastic essays impress no one; examiners smell padding instantly.
words.note: From bombast — cotton stuffing once used to pad clothing.

options: Believing people act only from self-interest
words.usage: The cynical narrator assumes the charity is a tax dodge — until the last paragraph.
words.note: From the Cynics, Greek philosophers who scorned conventional values.

options: A ruler with absolute, oppressive power
words.usage: The hostel warden ran lights-out like a benevolent despot.
words.note: Greek despotes = master of the household.

idioms.caption: The boat waited. Briefly.
options: Lose an opportunity by being too slow
idioms.origin: Literal origin — arrive late at the dock and watch your ship leave without you.
idioms.usage: Registration closed Tuesday; he checked Wednesday and missed the boat.

idioms.caption: Straight through the corner. What could go wrong.
options: Do something cheaply or carelessly to save effort
idioms.origin: From driving — swinging across a corner instead of taking the full, proper turn.
idioms.usage: Cut corners in Gate 1 checks and a broken payload reaches students — we learned that once.

words.usage: She read the tough Physics RC with total equanimity — panic is a time tax.
words.note: Latin aequus = even + animus = mind. An even mind.

options: Secretive; trying not to be noticed
words.usage: A furtive glance at the toppers' answer sheet — spotted instantly by the invigilator.
words.note: Latin fur = thief. Moving like a thief.

words.usage: His grandiose plan — 'finish Norman Lewis in a weekend' — lasted four pages.
words.note: Italian grandioso, from Latin grandis = great.

words.usage: The hapless student's laptop updated itself mid-mock — twice.
words.note: From hap = luck (as in 'happen', 'perhaps'). Luck-less.

words.usage: The +4/−1 rule is immutable; your attempt strategy is not.
words.note: Latin in + mutare = not changeable.

idioms.caption: The music was loud, the wagon was moving, everyone jumped.
options: Join something because it is popular
idioms.origin: From American election parades — politicians literally climbed onto the circus bandwagon to be seen with a winning candidate.
idioms.usage: Half the batch jumped on the 'question-first RC' bandwagon; for Indore, it backfired.

idioms.idiom: Take with a grain of salt
idioms.caption: Season the claim before swallowing it.
options: Treat a claim with healthy skepticism
idioms.origin: From Pliny the Elder — an ancient antidote recipe was to be taken with a grain of salt.
idioms.usage: Take 'this year's paper will be easy' predictions with a grain of salt.

options: Showing good judgment; wise and careful
words.usage: A judicious skip is worth more than a brave wrong answer.
words.note: Latin judicium = judgment. Note: 'judicial' = of courts; 'judicious' = wise.

options: To grow weak; to be neglected
words.usage: Unrevised words languish in notebooks; the Vault exists so they don't.
words.note: Latin languere = to be faint.

words.usage: Young opinions are malleable — which is why the author fears early propaganda.
words.note: Latin malleus = hammer. Metal soft enough to hammer into shape.

words.usage: 'Do better next time' is nebulous advice; 'cut RC guess-attempts to two' is not.
words.note: Latin nebula = mist, cloud.

options: Stubbornly refusing to change one's position
words.usage: Obstinate about his 'lucky pen', he nearly missed the OMR deadline refilling it.
words.note: Latin obstinare = to persist stubbornly.

idioms.caption: The bath was not optional.
idioms.origin: In English since the 1500s — the image of being scalded as punishment.
idioms.usage: Caught sharing the test link outside the batch, he was in hot water with admin.

idioms.caption: Admiral Nelson's telescope, wrong eye, on purpose.
idioms.origin: Admiral Nelson, blind in one eye, raised his telescope to it at Copenhagen and claimed he saw no signal to retreat.
idioms.usage: The warden turned a blind eye to the 2 am study group — marks were improving.

words.usage: The paucity of direct questions in Indore papers is exactly why inference practice matters.
words.note: Latin paucus = few.

options: Representing the most perfect example of something
words.usage: The quintessential Rohtak question: fact, stated verbatim, hiding in paragraph two.
words.note: Medieval 'fifth essence' — the purest element beyond earth, air, fire, water.

options: Able to recover quickly from setbacks
words.usage: Resilient isn't 'never falling'; it's a shorter gap between mock disaster and next attempt.
words.note: Latin resilire = to leap back.

words.usage: A sagacious mentor asks what you did in the last ten minutes of the paper.
words.note: Latin sagax = keen-scented, like a tracking dog.

options: A long, angry speech of criticism
words.usage: The coach's tirade about silly errors lasted longer than the test itself.
words.note: French tirer = to draw out — a long 'pull' of words.

idioms.caption: Friendship, meet public transport.
idioms.origin: Modern idiom from 1980s politics and journalism — sacrificing an ally for self-preservation.
idioms.usage: When the project flopped, he threw his teammate under the bus in front of the mentor.

idioms.idiom: Wear your heart on your sleeve
idioms.caption: Emotions: now available in outerwear.
idioms.origin: From medieval jousting, where knights tied a lady's ribbon to their sleeve; Shakespeare's Iago made the phrase famous.
idioms.usage: She wears her heart on her sleeve — you know her mock score from her walk.
